danger_signals.visual_form: Half-buried urns scattered across the game world
danger_signals.meaning: Kicking urns triggers dangerous, experience-pointless enemies (e.g., canine head that shoots eye bolts); signals environmental hazard and trap mechanics

danger_signals.visual_form: Tall black figure rising from ground with ominous musical cue
danger_signals.meaning: Unexpected difficult enemy encounter; audio-visual warning of high danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemies that zigzag sporadically at high speed, moving faster than player party can track
danger_signals.meaning: Aggressive, difficult-to-predict foes; visual indicator of combat difficulty
Party can be attacked while stationary during night observation; indicates vulnerability during certain world states

danger_signals.visual_form: Diverse enemy designs with distinct silhouettes and movement profiles
danger_signals.meaning: Surreal, unpredictable enemy encounters; visual diversity signals early-game brutality and design challenge

safety_signals.visual_form: Distinct architectural structures that can be revisited
safety_signals.meaning: Safe, self-contained level spaces functioning as sanctuaries (analogous to Zelda temples); offer loot and progression checkpoints

safety_signals.visual_form: Non-combat establishment where items can be purchased
safety_signals.meaning: Safe trading hub; indicates non-hostile settlement space

safety_signals.visual_form: NPCs that ambush party with item trading options
safety_signals.meaning: Commercial interaction opportunity; merchants signal non-lethal encounter despite 'ambush' framing

safety_signals.visual_form: Religious structures marked as pilgrimage destinations
safety_signals.meaning: Revival and healing locations; spiritual safe zones for party restoration

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Holy temple architecture and religious symbolism
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Anak holy temples scattered across world as pilgrimage destinations; function as spiritual sanctuaries and resurrection points

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Normal exploration lighting and environmental visibility
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Standard chance encounters with hostile monsters; party can move freely
Party becomes vulnerable to attacks while stationary; altered encounter conditions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dynamic lighting and sky changes throughout game session
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Behavior and encounter rates shift based on time of day; world feels temporally responsive

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character status indicators showing deceased party members
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Party at 'crippling disadvantage'; visual and mechanical incentive to pilgrimage to Anak holy temples for revival

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Self-contained architectural spaces with distinct visual layout from overworld
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Maze-like structures with obstacles, loot, and enemies; requires tactical navigation; combat mandatory within castles

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 3D hybrid landscape using vectors and character-scaling algorithms; freely traversable island
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Real-time encounter system; battles can occur while stationary; flee mechanic available outside castles

environmental_storytelling.example: Half-buried urns scattered across terrain function as trap indicators; their presence signals areas of danger and consequence-rich interaction
Surreal, diverse enemy variants (over 100) tell story of a dangerous, unpredictable world; early-game brutality communicates harsh environment through repeated lethal encounters
Anak holy temples visually differentiate from overworld; their placement and function as resurrection points establish religious faction power and world geography
Night sky observation creates vulnerability windows; animated day-night cycle makes world feel dynamic and inhabited by time-aware entities
Text-adventure log window (predating Ultima Underworld) acts as 'dungeon master,' providing narrative framing for damage, actions, and outcomes; establishes Gothic tabletop RPG aesthetic

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Regular chance encounters during overworld traversal; can attack party while stationary; automated by default but allow player micromanagement of tactics
Shoots bolts from eyes; stationary position; grants no experience points; quickly fatal to unprepared players
Unexpectedly lifts itself from ground with ominous musical accompaniment; difficult encounter; signals danger through audio-visual surprise
Zigzag movement at speeds exceeding player party movement; unpredictable pattern requiring tactical evasion or defensive magic
Create 'crippling disadvantage' condition; visual status indicator communicates urgency to pilgrimage to holy temple for revival
Ambush party with commercial intent; non-hostile despite 'ambush' framing; offer item trading transactions

notes: Drakkhen (SNES 1991) uses hybrid 3D rendering (vectors + character-scaling) to communicate a surreal, hostile world. Visual communication relies heavily on: (1) enemy design diversity to signal unpredictability and brutality, (2) urn hazards as environmental narrative, (3) architectural differentiation (castles vs. overworld) to establish safe vs. unsafe zones, (4) time-of-day state changes affecting vulnerability, and (5) interface-as-narrative (text log) for tactical communication. The SNES version de-emphasized PC version's text clutter in favor of visual means, condensing UI to bottom of screen. The game lacks explicit faction visualization but establishes Anak religious faction through temple placement and resurrection mechanics. No documented color-coded factions or sigils; world state communication is primarily behavioral (encounter conditions) and architectural (safe zone differentiation).
